sp.l: You may miſtake though. Can you juſtifie
On any cauſe, this ſeeking to deface,
The divine Image in a man?

sp.speaker: Bia.
sp.l: O Sir!
Let 'hem alone: Is not Diaphanous
As much a divine Image, as is Ironſide?
Let Images fight, if they will fight, a God's name.

div.head: ACT III. SCENE VI.
Keepe. Needle. Intereſt. To them inner 〈◊〉

sp.speaker: Kee.
sp.l: Where's Mr. Needle? Saw you Mr. Needle?
Wee are undone.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: What ailes the frantick Nurſe?

sp.speaker: Kee.
sp.l: My Miſtris is undone, ſhee's crying out!
Where is this man trow? Mr. Needle?

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: Here.

sp.speaker: Kee.
sp.l: Run for the party, Mrs. Chaire the Mid-wife.
Nay, looke how the man ſtands, as he were gok't!
Shee's loſt, if you not haſte away the party.

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: Where is the Doctor?

sp.speaker: Kee.
sp.l: Where a ſcoffing man is.
And his Apothecary, little better;
They laugh, and geere at all: will you diſpatch?
And fetch the party quickly to our Miſtris:
Wee are all undone! The Timpanie will out elſe.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Newes, newes, good newes, better then butter'd newes!
My Neice is found with Child, the Doctor tels me,
Exit. And falne in labour.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: How?

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: The portion's paid!
The portion—o'the Captaine! Is he here?

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: H'has ſpi'd your ſwords out! put 'hem up, put up,
Yo' have driven him hence; and yet your quarrell's ended.

sp.speaker: Iro.
sp.l: In a moſt ſtrange diſcovery.

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: Of light gold.

sp.speaker: Dia.
sp.l: And crack't within the Ring. I take the Omen,
As a good Omen.

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: Then put up your Sword,
And on your Doublet. Give the Captaine thankes.

sp.speaker: Dia.
sp.l: I had beene ſlur'd elſe. Thanke you noble Captaine!
Your quarrelling caus'd all this.

sp.speaker: Iro.
sp.l: Where's Compaſſe?

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: Gone,
Shrunke hence,! contracted to his Center, I feare.

sp.speaker: Iro.
sp.l: The ſlip is his then.

sp.speaker: Dia.
sp.l: I had like t'have beene
Abus'd i' the buſines, had the ſlip ſlur'd on me,
A Counterfeit.

sp.speaker: Bias.
sp.l: Sir, we are all abus'd:
As many as were brought on to be Suitors;
And we will joyne in thankes, all to the Captaine,
And to his fortune that ſo brought us off.

sp.speaker: Dam.
sp.p: This was a pittifull poore ſhift o' your Poët, Boy, to make his prime woman with child, and fall in labour, juſt to compoſe a quarrell.

sp.speaker: Boy.
sp.p: With whoſe borrowed eares, have you heard, Sir, all this while, that you can miſtake the current of our Scene ſo? The ſtreame of the Argument, threatned her being with child from the very beginning, for it preſented her in the firſt of the ſecond Act, with ſome apparent note of infirmity, or defect: from knowledge of which, the Auditory were rightly to bee ſuſpended by the Author, till the quarrell, which was but the accidentall cauſe, haſtned on the diſcovery of it, in occaſioning her affright; which made her fall into her throwes preſently, and within that compaſſe of time allow'd to the Comedy, wherein the Poët expreſt his prime Artifice, rather then any errour, that the detection of her being with child, ſhould determine the quarrell, which had produc'd it.

sp.speaker: Pro.
sp.p: The Boy is too hard for you. Brother Damplay, beſt marke the Play, and let him alone.

sp.speaker: Dam.
sp.p: I care not for marking the Play: Ile damne it, talke, and doe that I come for. I will not have Gentlemen loſe their priviledge, nor I my ſelfe my prerogative, for neere an overgrowne, or ſuperannuated Poët of 'hem all. Hee ſhall not give me the Law; I will cenſure, and be witty, and take my Tobacco, and enjoy my Magna Charta of reprehenſion, as my Predeceſſors have done before me.

sp.speaker: Boy.
sp.p: Even to licenſe, and abſurdity.

sp.speaker: Pro.
sp.p: Not now, becauſe the Gentlewoman is in travell: and the Midwife may come on the ſooner, to put her and us out of our paine.

sp.speaker: Dam.
sp.p:
Well, looke to your buſines afterward, Boy, that all things bee
cleare, and come properly forth, ſuited, and ſet together; for I will ſearch what followes ſeverely and to the naile.

sp.speaker: Boy.
sp.p: Let your naile run ſmooth then, and not ſcratch: leſt the Author be bold to pare it to the quick, and make it ſmart: you'l find him as ſevere as your ſelfe.

sp.speaker: Dam.
sp.p: A ſhrewd Boy! and has mee every where. The Mid-wife is come, ſhe has made haſte.

sp.speaker: CHa.
sp.l: Stay Mr. Needle, you doe prick too faſt
Vpo' the buſines: I muſt take ſome breath:
Lend me my ſtoole, you ha' drawne a ſtitch upon me,
In faith, ſonne Needle, with your haſte.

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: Good Mother, peice up this breach; Ile gi' you a new Gowne,
A new ſilke-Grogoran Gowne. Ile do't Mother.

sp.speaker: Kee.
sp.l: What'll you doe? you ha' done too much already
With your prick-ſeame, and through-ſtitch. Mr. Needle,
I pray you ſit not fabling here old tales,
Good Mother Chaire, the Mid-wife, but come up.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: How now Nurſe, where's my Lady?

sp.speaker: Kee.
sp.l: In her Chamber
Lock'd up, I thinke: ſhee'll ſpeake with no body.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Knowes ſhee o' this accident?

sp.speaker: Kee.
sp.l: Alas Sir, no;
Would ſhe might never know it.

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: I thinke her Ladiſhip
Too vertuous, and too nobly innocent,
To have a hand in ſo ill-form'd a buſines.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Your thought Sir is a brave thought, and a ſafe one,
The child now to be borne is not more free,
From the aſperſion of all ſpot, then ſhe?
She have her hand in plot, gainſt Mr. Practiſe.
If there were nothing elſe, whom ſhe ſo loves?
Cries up, and values? knowes to be a man
Mark'd out, for a chiefe Juſtice in his cradle?
Or a Lord Paramount; the head o' the Hall?
The Top or the Top-gallant of our Law?
Aſſure your ſelfe, ſhe could not ſo deprave,
The rectitude of her Judgement, to wiſh you
Vnto a wife, might prove your Infamy,
Whom ſhe eſteem'd that part o' the Common-wealth,
And had up for honour to her blood.

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: I muſt confeſſe a great beholdingneſſe
Vnto her Ladiſhips offer, and good wiſhes.
But the truth is, I never had affection,
Or any liking to this Neice of hers.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: You fore-ſaw ſomewhat then?

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: I had my notes,
And my Prognoſticks.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: You read Almanacks,
And ſtudy 'hem to ſome purpoſe, I beleeve?

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: I doe confeſſe, I doe beleeve, and pray too:
According to the Planets at ſometimes.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: And doe obſerve the ſigne in making Love?

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: As in Phlebotomy.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: And chooſe your Miſtris
By the good dayes, and leave her by the bad?

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: I doe, and I doe not.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: A little more
Would fetch all his Aſtronomie from Alleſtree.

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: I tell you Mr. Compaſſe, as my friend,
And under ſeale, I caſt mine eye long ſince,
Vpo' the other wench, my Ladies woman,
Another manner of peice for handſomneſſe,
Then is the Neice (but that is ſub ſigillo,
And as I give it you) in hope o' your aid,
And counſell in the buſines.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: You need counſell?
The only famous Counſell, o' the kingdome,
And in all Courts? That is a Jeere in faith,
Worthy your name, and your profeſſion too,
Sharpe Mr. Practiſe.

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: No, upo' my Law,
As I am a Bencher, and now double Reader,
I meant in meere ſimplicity of requeſt.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: If you meant ſo. Th'affaires are now perplex'd,
And full of trouble, give 'hem breath, and ſetling,
Ile doe my beſt. But in meane time doe you
Prepare the Parſon. (I am glad to know
This; for my ſelfe lik'd the young Maid before,
And lov'd her too.) Ha' you a Licence?

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: No;
But I can fetch one ſtraight.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Doe, doe, and mind
The Parſons pint t'ingage him—the buſines;
A knitting Cup there muſt be.

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: I ſhall doe it.

sp.speaker: Bia.
sp.l: Tis an affront, from you Sir; you here brought me,
Vnto my Ladies, and to wooe a wife,
Which ſince is prov'd a crack'd commoditie;
Shee hath broke bulke too ſoone.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: No fault of mine,
If ſhe be crack'd in peeces, or broke round;
It was my ſiſters fault, that ownes the houſe,
Where ſhe hath got her clap, makes all this noiſe.
I keepe her portion ſafe, that is not ſcatter'd:
The money's rattle not; nor are they throwne,
To make a Muſſe, yet 'mong the game ſome Suitors.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Can you endure that flout, cloſe Mr. Bias,
And have beene ſo bred in the Politiques?
The injury is done you, and by him only;
He lent you impreſt money, and upbraids it:
Furniſh'd you for the wooing, and now waves you.

sp.speaker: Bia.
sp.l: That makes me to expoſtulate the wrong
So with him, and reſent it as I doe.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: But doe it home then,

sp.speaker: Bia.
sp.l: Sir, my Lord ſhall know it.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: And all the Lords o' the Court too.

sp.speaker: Bia.
sp.l: What a Moath
You are Sir Intereſt!

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Wherein I intreat you,
Sweet Maſter Bias?

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: To draw in young Stateſ-men,
And heires of policie into the nooſe
Of an infamous matrimonie.

sp.speaker: Bia.
sp.l: Yes,
Infamous, quaſi in communem famam:
And Matrimony, quaſi, matter of Money.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Learnedly urg'd, my cunning Mr. Bias.

sp.speaker: Bia.
sp.l: With his lewd, knowne, and proſtituted Neice.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: My knowne, and proſtitute: how you miſtake,
And run upon a falſe ground, Mr. Bias!
(Your Lords will doe me right.) Now, ſhe is proſtitute,
And that I know it (pleaſe you underſtand me.)
I meane to keepe the portion in my hands:
And pay no monies.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Marke you that Don Bias?
And you ſhall ſtill remaine in bonds to him,
For wooing furniture, and impreſt charges.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Good Mr. Compaſſe, for the ſummes he has had
Of me, I doe acquit him: They are his owne.
Here, before you, I doe releaſe him.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Good!

sp.speaker: Bia.
sp.l: O Sir.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: 'Slid take it: I doe witneſſe it:
Hee cannot hurle away his money better.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: He ſhall get ſo much Sir, by my acquaintance,
To be my friend: And now report to his Lords
As I deſerve no otherwiſe.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: But well:
And I will witneſſe it, and to the value;
Foure hundred is the price, if I miſtake not,
Of your true friend in Court. Take hands, you ha' bought him,
And bought him cheap.

sp.speaker: Bia.
sp.l: I am his worſhips ſervant.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: And you his ſlave, Sir Moath. Seal'd, and deliver'd.
Ha' you not ſtudied the Court Complement?
Here are a paire of Humours, reconcil'd now,
That money held at diſtance: or their thoughts,
Baſer then money.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Out thou catife witch!
Baud, Beggar, Gipſey: Any thing indeed,
But honeſt woman.

sp.speaker: Kee.
sp.l: What you pleaſe, Dame Poliſh,
My Ladies Stroaker.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: What is here to doe?
The Goſſips out!

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Thou art a Traytor to me,
An Eve, the Apul, and the Serpent too:
A Viper, that haſt eat a paſſage through me,
Through mine owne bowels, by thy retchleſneſſe.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: What frantick fit is this? Ile ſtep aſide
And hearken to it.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Did I truſt thee, wretch,
With ſuch a ſecret, of that conſequence,
Did ſo concerne me, and my child, our livelihood,
And reputation? And haſt thou undone us?
By thy connivence, nodding in a corner,
And ſuffering her begot with child ſo baſely?
Sleepie unlucky Hag! Thou bird of night,
And all miſchance to me.

sp.speaker: Kee.
sp.l: Good Lady Empreſſe!
Had I the keeping of your Daughters clicket
In charge? was that committed to my truſt?

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Her Daughter.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Softly Divell, not ſo low'd,
You'ld ha' the houſe heare, and be witneſſe, would you?

sp.speaker: Kee.
sp.l: Let all the world be witneſſe. Afore Ile
Endure the Tyrannie of ſuch a tongue—
And ſuch a pride—.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: What will you doe?

sp.speaker: Kee.
sp.l: Tell truth,
And ſhame the She-man-Divell in puff'd ſleeves;
Run any hazzard, by revealing all
Vnto my Lady: how you chang'd the cradles,
And chang'd the children in 'hem.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Not ſo high!

sp.speaker: Kee.
sp.l: Calling your Daughter Pleaſance, there Placentia,
And my true Miſtris by the name of Pleaſance.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: A horrid ſecret, this! worth the diſcovery;

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: And muſt you be thus lowd?

sp.speaker: Kee.
sp.l: I will be lowder:
And cry it through the houſe, through every roome,
And every office of the Lawndry-maids:
Till it be borne hot to my Ladies eares.
Ere I will live in ſuch a ſlavery,
Ile doe away my ſelfe.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Didſt thou not ſweare
To keepe it ſecret? and upon what booke?
(I doe remember now) The Practice of Piety.

sp.speaker: Kee.
sp.l: It was a practice of impiety,
Out of your wicked forge, I know it now,
My conſcience tels me. Firſt, againſt the Infants,
To rob them o' their names, and their true parents;
T' abuſe the neighbour-hood, keepe them in errour;
But moſt my Lady: Shee has the maine wrong:
And I wil let her know it inſtantly.
Repentance, (if it be true) nere comes too late.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: What have I done? Conjur'd a ſpirit up
I ſha' not lay againe? drawne on a danger,
And ruine on my ſelfe thus, by provoking
A peeviſh foole, whom nothing will pray of,
Or ſatisfie I feare? Her patience ſtirr'd,
Is turn'd to fury. I have run my Barke,
On a ſweet Rock, by mine owne arts, and truſt:
And muſt get off againe, or daſh in peeces.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: This was a buſines, worth the liſtning after.

sp.speaker: Ple.
sp.l: O Mr. Compaſſe, did you ſee my Mother?
Miſtris Placentia, my Ladies Neice;
Is newly brought to bed o'the braveſt boy!
Will you goe ſee it?

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Firſt, Ile know the father,
Ere I approach theſe hazards.

sp.speaker: Ple.
sp.l: Miſtris Midwife
Has promis'd to find out a father for it,
If there be need.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Shee may the ſafelier do't,
By vertue of her place. But pretty Pleaſance,
I have a newes for you, I thinke will pleaſe you.

sp.speaker: Ple.
sp.l: What is't Mr. Compaſſe?

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Stay, you muſt
Deſerve it ere you know it. Where's my Lady?

sp.speaker: Ple.
sp.l: Retir'd unto her Chamber, and ſhut up.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: She heares o' none o' this yet? well, doe you
Command the Coach; and fit your ſelfe to travell?
A little way with me.

sp.speaker: Ple.
sp.l: Whither, for Gods ſake.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Where Ile intreat you not to your loſſe, beleeve it.
If you dare truſt your ſelfe.

sp.speaker: Ple.
sp.l: With you the world ore.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: The newes will well requite the paines, I aſſure you.
And i' this tumult you will not be miſt.
Command the Coach, it is an inſtant buſines,
Wu' not be done without you. Parſon Palate
Moſt opportunely met, ſtep to my Chamber:
Ile come to you preſently. There is a friend,
Or two, will entertaine you, Mr. Practiſe.
Ha' you the Licence?

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: Here it is.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Let's ſee it:
Your name's not in't.

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: Ile fill that preſently;
It has the Seale, which is the maine: And reſtgiſtred,
The Clarke knowes me, and truſts me.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Ha' you the Parſon?

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: They ſay hee's here, he' pointed to come hither.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: I would not have him ſeene here for a world,
To breed ſupition. Doe you intercept him,
And prevent that. But take your Licence with you,
And fill the blanke: or leave it here with me,
Ile doe it for you, ſtay you with us at his Church,
Behind the old Exchange, wee'll come i'th Coach,
And meet you there within this Quarter at leaſt.

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: I am much bound unto you, Mr. Compaſſe,
You have all the Law, and parts of Squire Practiſe
For ever at your uſe. Ile tell you newes, too:
Sir, your Reverſion's fall'n: Thin-wits dead,
Surveyor of the Projects generall.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: When died he?

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: Een • this morning, I receiv'd it
From a right hand.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Conceale it Mr. Practiſe,
And mind the maine affaire, you are in hand with.

sp.speaker: Ple.
sp.l: The Coach is ready Sir.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: 'Tis well faire Pleaſance,
Though now wee ſhall not uſe it, bid the Coach-man
Drive to the Pariſh Church, and ſtay about there,
Till Mr. Practiſe come to him, and imploy him:
I have a Licence now, which muſt have entry
Before my Lawyers. Noble Parſon Palate,
Thou ſhalt be a marke advanc't: here's a peece,
And doe a feat for me.

sp.speaker: Pal.
sp.l: What, Mr. Compaſſe?

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: But run the words of Matrimony, over
My head, and Mrs. Pleaſances in my Chamber:
There's Captaine Ironſide to be a witneſſe:
And here's a Licence to ſecure thee. Parſon!
What doe you ſtick at?

sp.speaker: Pal.
sp.l: It is after-noone Sir,
Directly againſt the Canon of the Church;
You know it Mr. Compaſſe: and beſide,
I am ingag'd unto our worſhipfull friend,
The learned Mr. Practiſe in that buſines.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Come on, ingage yourſelfe: Who ſhall be able
To ſay you married us, but i'the morning,
The moſt canonicall minute o'the day,
If you affirme it? That's a ſpic'd excuſe,
And ſhewes you have ſet the Common Law, before
Any profeſſion elſe, of love, or friendſhip.
Come Mrs. Pleaſance, wee cannot prevaile
With th' rigid Parſon here; but Sir, Ile keepe you
Lock'd in my lodging, 'till't be done elſewhere,
And under feare of Ironſide.

sp.speaker: Pal.
sp.l: Doe you heare, Sir?

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: No, no, it matters not.

sp.speaker: Pal.
sp.l: Can you thinke Sir
I would deny you my thing? not to loſſe
Of both my Livings: I will doe it for you,
Ha' you a wedding Ring?

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: I and a Poeſie:

l.hi:
Annulus hic nobis, quod ſcit uter
que dabit.

sp.speaker: Pal.
sp.l: Good!
This Ring will give you what you both deſire.
Ile make the whole houſe chant it, and the Pariſh.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Why, well ſaid Parſon. Now to you my newes,
That comprehend my reaſons, Mrs. Pleaſance.

sp.speaker: Cha.
sp.l: Goe, get a Nurſe, procure her at what rate
You can: and out o'th'houſe with it, ſonne Needle.
It is a bad Commoditie.

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: Good Mother,
I know it, but the beſt would now be made on't.

sp.speaker: Cha.
sp.l: And ſhall: you ſhould not fret ſo, Mrs. Poliſh,
Nor you Dame Keepe; my Daughter ſhall doe well,
When ſhe has tane my Cawdle. I ha' knowne
Twenty ſuch breaches piec'd up, and made whole,
Without a bum of noiſe. You two fall out?
And teare up one another?

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Bleſſed woman?

sp.speaker: Kee.
sp.l: The Peaſe-dreſſer!
Ile heare no peace from her. I have beene wrong'd,
So has my Lady, my good Ladies worſhip,
And I will right her, hoping ſhee'll right me.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Good gentle Keepe, I pray thee Miſtris Nurſe,
Pardon my paſſion, I was miſadvis'd,
Be thou yet better, by this grave ſage woman,
Who is the Mother of Matrons, and great perſons,
And knowes the world.

sp.speaker: Kee.
sp.l: I doe confeſſe, ſhe knowes
Something—and I know ſomething—.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Put your ſomethings.
Together then.

sp.speaker: Cha.
sp.l: I, here's a chance falne out
You cannot helpe; leſſe can this Gentlewoman;
I can and will, for both. Firſt, I have ſent
By-chop away; the cauſe gone, the fame ceaſeth.
Then by my Cawdle, and my Cullice, I ſet
My Daughter on her feet, about the houſe here:
Shee's young, and muſt ſtirre ſomewhat for neceſſity,
Her youth will beare it out. She ſhall pretend,
T'have had a fit o'the Mother: there is all.
If you have but a Secretary Landreſſe,
To blanch the Linnen—Take the former counſels
Into you; keepe them ſafe i' your owne breſts;
And make your Merkat of 'hem at the higheſt.
Will you goe peach, and cry yourſelfe a foole
At Granam's Croſſe? be laugh'd at, and diſpis'd▪
Betray a purpoſe, which the Deputie
Of a double Ward, or ſcarce his Alderman,
With twelve of the wiſeſt Queſtmen could find out,
Imployed by the Authority of the Citie?
Come, come, be friends: and keepe theſe women-matters,
Smock-ſecrets to our ſelves, in our owne verge.
Wee ſhall marre all, if once we ope the myſteries
O' the Tyring-houſe, and tell what's done within:
No Theaters are more cheated with apparances,
Or theſe ſhop-lights, then th' Ages, and folke in them,
That ſeeme moſt curious.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Breath of an Oracle!
You ſhall be my deare Mother; wiſeſt woman
That ever tip'd her tongue, with point of reaſons,
To turne her hearers! Miſtris Keepe, relent,
I did abuſe thee; I confeſſe to pennance:
And on my knees aske thee forgiveneſſe.

sp.speaker: Cha.
sp.l: Riſe,
She doth begin to melt, I ſee it—.

sp.speaker: Kee.
sp.l: Nothing
Griev'd me ſo much, as when you call'd me Baud:
Witch did not trouble me, nor Gipſie; no
Nor Beggar. But a Baud, was ſuch a name!

sp.speaker: Cha.
sp.l: No more rehearſals; Repetitions
Make things the worſe: The more wee ſtirre (you know
The Proverbe, and it ſignifies a) ſtink.
What's done, and dead, let it be buried.
New houres will fit freſh handles, to new thoughts.

div.head: ACT IV. SCENE VIII.
Intereſt, with his Foot-boy. To them Compaſſe. Ironſide. Silkeworme. Palate. Pleaſance. To them the Lady: and after Practiſe.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Run to the Church, Sirrah. Get all the Drunkards
To ring the Bels, and jangle them for joy
My Neice hath brought an Heire unto the houſe,
A luſty boy. Where's my ſiſter Loadſtone?
A ſleepe at afternoones! It is not wholeſome;
Againſt all rules of Phyſick, Lady ſiſter.
The little Doctor will not like it. Our Neice
Is new deliver'd of a chopping Child,
Can call the Father by the name already,
If it but ope the mouth round. Mr. Compaſſe,
He is the man, they ſay, fame gives it out,
Hath done that Act of honour to our houſe,
And friendſhip to pompe out a Sonne, and Heire,
That ſhall inherit nothing, ſurely nothing
From me at leaſt. I come t' invite your Ladiſhip
To be a witneſſe; I will be your Partner,
And give it a horne-ſpoone, and a treene diſh;
Baſtard, and Beggars badges, with a blanket
For Dame the Doxey to march round the Circuit,
With bag, and baggage.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Thou malitious Knight,
Envious Sir Moath, that eates on that which feeds thee,
And frets her goodneſſe, that ſuſtaines thy being;
What company of Mankind would owne thy brother-hood,
But as thou haſt a title to her blood,
Whom thy ill nature hath choſe out t'inſult on,
And vexe thus, for an Accident in her houſe,
As if it were her crime! Good innocent Lady,
Thou ſhew'ſt thy ſelfe a true corroding Vermine,
Such as thou art.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Why, gentle Mr. Compaſſe?
Becauſe I wiſh you joy of your young Sonne,
And Heire to the houſe, you ha' ſent us?

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: I ha' ſent you?
I know not what I ſhall doe. Come in friends:
Madam, I pray you be pleas'd to truſt your ſelfe
Vnto our company.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: I did that too late;
Which brought on this calamity upon me,
With all the infamy I heare; your Souldier,
That ſwaggering Gueſt.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Who is return'd here to you,
Your vowed friend, and ſervant; comes to ſup with you,
So wee doe all; and 'll prove he hath deſerv'd,
That ſpeciall reſpect, and favour from you,
As not your fortunes, with your ſelfe to boote,
Caſt on a Feather-bed, and ſpread o'th' ſheets
Vnder a brace of your beſt Perſian Carpets,
Were ſcarce a price to thanke his happy merit.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: What impudence is this? can you indure
To heare it ſiſter?

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Yes, and you ſhall heare it;
Who will indure it worſe. What deſerves he
In your opinion, Madam, or weigh'd Judgement,
That, things thus hanging (as they doe in doubt)
Suſpended, and ſuſpected, all involv'd,
And wrapt in errour, can reſolve the knot?
Redintigrate the fame, firſt of your houſe?
Reſtore your Ladiſhips quiet? render then
Your Neice a Virgin, and unvitiated?
And make all plaine, and perfect (as it was)
A practiſe to betray you, and your name?

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Hee ſpeakes impoſſibilities.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Here he ſtands,
Whoſe fortune hath done this, and you muſt thanke him:
To what you call his ſwaggering, wee owe all this.
And that it may have credit with you Madam,
Here is your Neice, whom I have married, witneſſe
Theſe Gentlemen, the Knight, Captaine, and Parſon,
And this grave Politique Tell-troth of the Court.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: What's ſhe that I call Neice then?

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Poliſhes Daugh
Her Mother Good wy' Poliſh hath confeſs'd it
To Granam Keepe, the Nurſe, how they did change
The children in their Cradles.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: To what purpoſe?

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: To get the portion, or ſome part of it,
Which you muſt now disburſe intire to me, Sir,
If I but gaine her Ladiſhips conſent.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: I bid God give you joy, if this be true.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: As true it is, Lady, Lady, i'th' ſong.
The portion's mine, with intereſt Sir Mouth;
I will not 'bate you a ſingle Harrington,
Of intereſt upon intereſt. In meane time,
I doe commit you to the Guard of Ironſide.
My brother here, Captaine Rudhudibras:
From whom I will expect you, or your Ranſome.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Sir you muſt prove it, and the poſſibility,
Ere I beleeve it.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: For the poſſibility,
I leave to triall. Truth ſhall ſpeake it ſelfe.
O Mr. Practiſe, did you meet the Coach?

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: Yes Sir, but empty.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Why, I ſent it for you.
The buſines is diſpatch'd here, ere you come;
Come in, Ile tell you how: you are a man
Will looke for ſatisfaction, and muſt have it.

sp.speaker: All.
sp.l: So doe wee all, and long to heare the right.

sp.speaker: Dam.
sp.p: Troth, I am one of thoſe that labour with the ſame longing, for it is almoſt pucker'd, and pull'd into that knot, by your Poët, which I cannot eaſily, with all the ſtrength of my imagination, untie.

sp.speaker: Boy.
sp.p: Like enough, nor is it in your office to be troubled or perplexed with it, but to ſit ſtill, and expect. The more your imagination buſies it ſelfe, the more it is intangled, eſpecially if (as I told, in the beginning) you happen on the wrong end.

sp.speaker: Pro.
sp.p: He hath ſaid ſufficient, Brother Damplay; our parts that are the Spectators, or ſhould heare a Comedy, are to await the proceſſe, and events of things, as the Poet preſents them, not as wee would corruptly faſhion them. Wee come here to behold Playes, and cenſure them, as they are made, and fitted for us; not to beſlave our owne thoughts, with cenſorious ſpitle tempering the Poets clay, as wee were to mould every Scene anew: That were a meere Plaſtick, or Potters ambition, moſt unbecomming the name of a Gentleman. No, let us marke, and not loſe the buſines on foot, by talking. Follow the right thred, or find it.

sp.speaker: Dam.
sp.p: Why, here his Play might have ended, if hee would ha' let it; and have ſpar'd us the vexation of a fift Act yet to come, which every one here knowes the iſſue of already, or may in part conjecture.

sp.speaker: Boy.
sp.p: That conjecture is a kind of Figure-flinging, or throwing the Dice, for a meaning was never in the Poets purpoſe perhaps. Stay, and ſee his laſt Act, his Cataſtrophe, how hee will perplexe that, or ſpring ſome freſh cheat, to entertaine the Spectators, with a convenient delight, till ſome unexpected, and new encounter breake out to rectifie all, and make good the Concluſion.

sp.speaker: Pro.
sp.p: Which, ending here, would have ſhowne dull, flat, and unpointed; without any ſhape, or ſharpeneſſe, Brother Damplay.

sp.speaker: Dam.
sp.l: Well, let us expect then: And wit be with us, o'the Poets part.

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: TRoth Mr. Item, here's a houſe divided,
And quarter'd into parts, by your Doctors ingine.
H'has caſt out ſuch aſperſions on my Ladies
Neice here, of having had a Child; as hardly
Will be wip'd off, I doubt.

sp.speaker: Ite.
sp.l: Why, is't not true?

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: True! did you thinke it?

sp.speaker: Ite.
sp.l: Was ſhee not in labour?
The Mid-wife ſent for?

sp.speaker: Ite.
sp.l: There's your errour now!
Yo' ha' drunke o' the ſame water.

sp.speaker: Item.
sp.l: I beleev'd it,
And gave it out too.

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: More you wrong'd the party;
She had no ſuch thing about her, innocent creature!

sp.speaker: Iem.
sp.l: What had ſhe then? only a fit o'the Mother,
They burnt old ſhoes, Gooſe-feathers, Aſſafoetida,
A few horne ſhavings, with a bone, or two,
And ſhe is well againe, about the houſe;—

sp.speaker: Ite.
sp.l: Is't poſſible?

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: See it, and then report it.

sp.speaker: Ite.
sp.l: Our Doctors Vrinall-Judgement is halfe-crack'd then.

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: Crack't i' the caſe, moſt hugely, with my Lady,
And ſad Sir Moath, her brother; who is now
Vnder a cloud a little.

sp.speaker: Ite.
sp.l: Of what? Diſgrace?

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: He is committed to Rud-hudibras,
The Captaine Ironſide, upon diſpleaſure,
From Mr. Compaſſe, but it will blow off.

sp.speaker: Ite.
sp.l: The Doctor ſhall reverſe his, inſtantly,
And ſet all right againe: if you'll aſſiſt
But in a toy; Squire Needle, comes i' my nodle now.

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: Good, Needle and Nodle! what may't be? I long for't.

sp.speaker: Ite.
sp.l: Why, but to goe to bed: faine a diſtemper
Of walking i'your ſleepe, or talking in't
A little idly, but ſo much, as on' it,
The Doctor may have ground, to raiſe a cure
For's reputation.

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: Any thing, to ſerve
The worſhip o' the man I love and honour.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: O! gi' you joy Madamoiſelle Compaſſe!
You are his Whirle-poole now: all to be married,
Againſt your Mothers leave, and without counſell!
H' has fiſh'd faire, and caught a Frog, I feare it.
What fortune ha' you to bring him in dower?
You can tell ſtories now: you know a world
Of ſecrets to diſcover.

sp.speaker: Ple.
sp.l: I know nothing
But what is told me; nor can I diſcover
Anything.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: No, you ſhall not, Ile take order.
Goe, get you in there: It is Ember-weeke!
Ile keepe you faſting from his fleſh a while.

sp.speaker: Cha.
sp.l: See, who's here? ſhe 'has beene with my Lady; who kiſt her, all to kiſt her, twice or thrice.

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: And call'd her Neice againe, and view'd her Linnen.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: You ha' done a Miracle, Mother Chaire.

sp.speaker: Cha.
sp.l: Not I,
My Cawdle has done it. Thanke my Cawdle heartily.

sp.speaker: Pol
sp.l: It ſhall be thank'd, and you too, wiſeſt Mother;
You ſhall have a new, brave, foure-pound Beaver hat,
Set with enamell'd ſtuds, as mine is here:
And a right paire of Criſtall Spectacles,
Criſtall o' th' Rock, thou mighty Mother of Dames,
Hung in an Ivory Caſe, at a gold Belt,
And ſilver Bels to gingle, as you paſe
Before your fiftie Daughters in proceſſion
To Church, or from the Church.

sp.speaker: Cha.
sp.l: Thankes Mrs. Poliſh.

sp.speaker: Kee.
sp.l: She does deſerve as many penſions,
As there be peeces in a—Maiden-head;
Were I a Prince to give 'hem.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Come ſweet Charge,
You ſhall preſent your ſelfe about the houſe, be confident, and beare up;
you ſhall be ſeene.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: What? I can make you amends, my learned Counſell,
And ſatisfie a greater Injury
To chafed Mr. Practiſe. Who would thinke
That you could be thus teſtie?

sp.speaker: Iro.
sp.l: A grave head!
Gi'n over to the ſtudy of our Lawes.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: And the prime honours of the Common-wealth.

sp.speaker: Iro.
sp.l: And you to mind a wiſe.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: What ſhould you doe
With ſuch a toy as a wife, that might diſtract you,
Or hinder you i' your Courſe?

sp.speaker: Iro.
sp.l: He ſhall not thinke on't.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: I will make over to you my Poſſeſſion,
Of that ſame place is falne (you know) to ſatisfie
Surveyor of the Projects generall.

sp.speaker: Iro.
sp.l: And that's an office, you know how to ſtirre in.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: And make your profits of.

sp.speaker: Iro.
sp.l: Which are (indeed)
The ends of a gown'd man: Shew your activity,
And how you are built for buſines.

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: I accept it
As a P • ſſeſsion, be't but a Reverſion.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: You firſt told me 'twas a Poſſeſſion.

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: I,
I told you that I heard ſo.

sp.speaker: Iro.
sp.l: All is one,
Hee'll make Reverſion a Poſſeſsion quickly.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: But I muſt have a generall Releaſe from you.

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: Doe one, He doe the other.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: It's a match
Before my brother Ironſide.

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: 'Tis done.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Wee two are reconcil'd then.

sp.speaker: Iro.
sp.l: To a Lawyer,
That can make uſe of a place, any halfe title,
Is better then a wife.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: And will ſave charges
Of Coaches, Vellute Gownes, and cut-worke Smocks.

sp.speaker: Iro.
sp.l: Hee is to occupie an office wholly.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: True, I muſt talke with you neerer, Mr. Practiſe,
About recovery o' my wives portion,
What way I were beſt to take.

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: The plaineſt way.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: What's that, for plaineneſſe?

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: Sue him at Common-Law:
Arreſt him on an Action of Choke-baile,
Five hundred thouſand pound; it will affright him,
And all his ſureties. You can prove your marriage?

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Yes.
Wee'll talke of it within, and heare my Lady.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: I'am ſure, the Rogue o' the houſe went all that way;
She was with Child, and Mr. Compaſſe got it.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: Why, that you ſee, is manifeſtly falſe,
H' has married the other; our true Neice he ſayes:
He would not wooe 'hem both: hee is not ſuch
A Stallion, to leape all. Againe, no Child
Appeares, that I can find with all my ſearch,
And ſtricteſt way of Inquiry, I have made
Through all my family. A fit o' the Mother,
The women ſay ſhe had, which the Mid-wife our'd,
With burning bones and feathers: Here's the Doctor. Enter Doctor.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: O noble Doctor, did not you, and your Item,
Tell me our Neice was in labour?

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: If I did,
What followes?

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: And that Mother Mid-night
Was ſent for?

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: So ſhe was; and is i'the houſe ſtill.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: But here has a noiſe beene ſince, ſhe was deliver'd
Of a brave boy, and Mr. Compaſſe's getting.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: I know no rattle of Goſſips, nor their noyſes.
I hope you take not me for a Pimpe errant,
To deale in ſmock Affaires? Where's the Patient?
The infirme man, I was ſent for, Squire Needle?

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: Is Needle ſick?

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: My 'Pothecary tels me Enter Tim.
Hee is in danger; how is't Tim? where is he?

sp.speaker: Ite.
sp.l: I cannot hold him downe. Hee's up, and walkes,
And talkes in his perfect ſleepe, with his eyes ſhut,
As ſenſibly, as he were broad awake.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: See, here becomes, Hee's faſt aſleepe, obſerve him.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Hee'll tell us wonders: What doe theſe women here?

div.head: ACT V. SCENE V.

head.hi: Rut. Needle. Intereſt. Item. Lady. Poliſh. Chaire. Keepe. Placentia.

div.l: Hunting a man halfe naked? you are fine beagles!

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: I ha' linnen breeks on.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: He heares, but hee ſees nothing.

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: Yes, I ſee
Who hides the treaſure yonder.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Ha? what treaſure?

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: If you aske queſtions, he 'wakes preſently:
And then you'l heare no more, till his next fit.

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: And whom ſhe hides it for.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Doe you marke Sir? Int.

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: A fine ſhe ſpirit it is, an Indian Mag-pie.
She was an Aldermans Widow, and fell in love
With our Sir Moath, my Ladies brother.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: (Heare you?)

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: And ſhe has hid an Aldermans eſtate;
Dropt through her bill in little holes, i' the Garden,
And ſcrapes earth over 'hem; where none can ſpy
But I, who ſee all by the Glowormes light,
That creeps before.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: I knew the Gentlewoman;
Alderman Parrots Widow, a fine Speaker,
As any was i' the Clothing, or the Bevy;
She did become her ſcarlet, and black Velvet,
Her greene, and purple—

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Save thy colours, Rainebow,
Or ſhe will run thee over, and all thy lights.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: She dwelt in Doo-little Lane, a top o'the hill there;
I' the round Cage, was after Sir Chime Squirrell's.
Shee would eate nought but Almonds, I aſſure you.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Would thou had'ſt a doſe of pilles, a double doſe,
O' the beſt purge, to make thee turne tale, tother way.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: You are a foule mouth'd, purging, abſurd Doctor;
I tell you true, and I did long to tell it you.
You ha'ſpread a ſcandall i' my Ladies houſe here,
On her ſweet Neice, you never can take off
With all your purges, or your plaiſter of Oathes;
Though you diſtill your Dam-me, drop by drop,
I' your defence. That ſhe hath had a Child,
Here ſhe doth ſpit upon thee, and defie thee;
Or I do't for her.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Madam, pray you bind her
To her behaviour. Tye your Goſſip up,
Or ſend her unto Bet'lem.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Goe thou thither,
That better haſt deſerv'd it, ſhame of Doctors:
Where could ſhe be deliver'd? by what charme?
Reſtor'd to her ſtrength ſo ſoone? who is the Father?
Or where the Infant? Aske your Oracle,
That walkes, and talkes in his ſleepe.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Where is he? gone?
You ha' loſt a fortune liſtning to her, to her Tabour.
Good Madam lock her up.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: You muſt give looſers
Their leave to ſpeake, good Doctor.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Follow his footing
Before he get to his bed: This reſt is loſt elſe.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Where is my wife? what ha' you done with my wife,
Goſſip o' the Counſels?

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: I, ſweet Mr. Compaſſe?
I honour you, and your wife.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Well, doe ſo ſtill.
I will not call you Mother tho', but Poliſh.
Good Goſſip Poliſh, where ha' you hid my wife?

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: I hide your wife?

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Or ſhe's run away.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: That would make all ſuſpected, Sir, a freſh.
Come we will find her, if ſhe be i' the houſe.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Why ſhould I hide your wife, good Mr. Compaſſe?

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: I know no cauſe, but that you are goo'dy Poliſh,
That's good at malice; good at miſchiefe; all
That can perplexe, or trouble a buſines, throughly.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: You may ſay what you will: yo' are Mr. Compaſſe,
And carry a large ſweep, Sir, i' your Circle.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: Ile ſweep all corners, Goſſip, to ſpring this.
If't be above ground, I will have her cry'd,
By the Common-cryer, through all the Ward,
But I will find her.

sp.speaker: Iro.
sp.l: It will be an Act
Worthy your juſtice, Madam.

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: And become
The integrity, and worſhip of her name.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: 'Tis ſuch a Fly, this Goſſip, with her buz,
Shee blowes on every thing, in every place!

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: A buſie woman, is a fearefull grievance!
Will hee not ſleepe againe?

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Yet inſtantly,
As ſoone as he is warme. It is the nature
Of the diſeaſe, and all theſe cold dry fumes,
That are melancholicke, to worke at firſt,
Slow, and inſenſibly in their aſcent,
Till being got up, and then diſtilling downe
Vpo' the braine; they have a pricking quality
That breeds this reſtleſſe reſt, which we, the ſonnes
Of Phyſick, call a walking in the ſleepe,
And telling myſteries, that muſt be heard.
Softly, with art, as we were ſowing pillowes
Vnder the Patients elbowes, elſe they'd fly
Into a phrenſie, run into the Woods,
Where there are Noiſes, huntings, ſhoutings, hallowings,
Amidſt the brakes, and furzes, over bridges
Fall into waters: Scratch their fleſh: Sometimes
Drop downe a praecipice, and there be loſt.
How now! what does her?

sp.speaker: Ite.
sp.l: He is up againe, Enter Item.
And 'gins to talke.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: O' the former matter, Item?

sp.speaker: Ite.
sp.l: The treaſure, and the Lady: That's his argument.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: O mee, happy man! he cannot off it.
I ſhall know all then.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: With what appetite
Our owne deſires delude us! Heare you Tim?
Let no man interrupt us.

sp.speaker: Ite.
sp.l: Sir Diaphanous,
And Mr. Bias, his Court-friend's, deſire
To kiſſe his Neices hands, and gratulate
The firme recovery of her good fame,
And honour—

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Good, ſay to 'hem, Mr. Item,
My Neice is on my Ladies ſide: they'll find her there.
I pray to be but ſpar'd, for halfe an houre:
Ile ſee 'hem preſently.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Doe, put 'hem off, Tim.
And tell 'hem the importance of the buſines.
Here, he is come! ſooth; and have all out of him.

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: How doe you Lady-bird? ſo hard at worke, ſtill?
What's that you ſay? Doe you bid me walke, ſweet Bird?
And tell our Knight? I will. How? walke knave, walke?
I thinke y' are angry with me

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Fine Pol!
Pol's a fine bird! O fine Lady Pol!
Almond for Parrat; Parrat's a brave bird:
Three hundred thouſand peeces ha' you ſtuck,
Edge-long into the ground, within the Garden?
O'bounteous Bird!

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: And me, moſt happy creature.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Smother your joy.

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: How? and drop'd twice ſo many—

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Ha! where?

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Containe your ſelfe.

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: I' the old Well?

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: I cannot, I am a man of fleſh, and blood:
Who can containe himſelfe, to heare the Ghoſt
Of a dead Lady, doe ſuch workes as theſe?
And a Citie Lady too, o'the ſtreight waſte?

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Hee's gone.

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: I will goe try the truth of it.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Follow him,

sp.speaker: Tim:
sp.l: See what he does; if he bring you
A'ſſay of it now.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Ile ſay hee's a rare fellow:
And has a rare diſeaſe.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: And I will worke
As rare a cure upon him.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: How, good Doctor?

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: When he hath utter'd all, that you would know of him;
Ile clenſe him with a pill (as ſmall as a peaſe)
And ſtop his mouth: for there his iſſue lies,
Betweene the Muſcles o'the tongue.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Hee's come.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: What did he, Item?

sp.speaker: Ite.
sp.l: The firſt ſtep he ſtept
Into the Garden, he pull'd theſe five peices
Vp, in a fingers breath one of another.
The durt ſticks on 'hem ſtill.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: I know enough.
Doctor, proceed with your Cure, Ile make thee famous,
Famous among the ſonnes of the Phyſicians,
Thou ſhalt have a golden beard, as well as he had;
And thy Tim Item here, have one of ſilver:
A livery beard. And all thy 'Pothecaries
Belong to thee. Where's Squire Needle? gone?

sp.speaker: Ite.
sp.l: Hee's prick'd away, now he has done the worke.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Prepare his pill, and gi' it him afore Supper.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Ile ſend for a dozen o'labourers to morrow,
To turne the ſurface o'the Garden up.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: In mould? bruiſe every clod?

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: And have all ſifted;
For Ile not looſe a peice o'the Birds bounty,
And take an Inventory of all.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: And then,
I would goe downe into the Well—

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: My ſelfe;
No truſting other hands: Sixe hundred thouſand,
To the firſt three; nine hundred thouſand pound—

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: 'Twill purchaſe the whole Bench of Aldermanity,
Stript to their ſhirts.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: There never did accrew,
So great a gift to man, and from a Lady,
I never ſaw but once; now I remember,
Wee met at Merchants▪ Taylors-hall, at dinner,
In Thred-needle ſtreet,

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Which was a ſigne Squire Needle
Should have the thredding of this thred.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: 'Tis true;
I ſhall love Parrots better, while I know him.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Il'd have her ſtatue cut, now in white marble.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: And have it painted in moſt orient colours.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: That's right! all Citie ſtatues muſt be painted:
Elſe, they be worth nought i' their ſubtile Judgements.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: My trueſt friend in Court, deare Mr. Bias;
You heare o'the recovery of our Neice
In fame, and credit?

sp.speaker: Bia.
sp.l: Yes, I have beene with her,
And gratulated to her; but I am ſory
To find the Author o' the fowle aſperſion
Here i' your company, this inſolent Doctor.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: You doe miſtake him: He is cleare got off on't.
A Goſſips Jealouſie firſt gave the hint.
He drives another way, now, as I would have him.
Hee's a rare man, the Doctor, in his way.
H' has done the nobleſt cure here, i' the houſe,
On a poore Squire, my ſiſters Taylor, Needle
That talk'd in's ſleepe; would walke to Saint Iohn's wood,
And Waltham Forreſt, ſcape by all the ponds,
And pits i'the way; run over two-inch bridges;
With his eyes faſt, and i'the dead of night!
Ile ha' you better acquainted with him. Doctor,
Here is my deare, deare, deareſt friend in Court,
Wiſe, powerfull Mr. Bias; pray you ſalute
Each other, not as ſtrangers, but true friends.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: This is the Gentleman you brought to day,
A Suitor to your Neice?

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Yes.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: You were
Agreed, I heard; the writings drawne betweene you?

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: And ſeald.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: What broke you off?

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: This rumour of her?
Was it not Mr. Bias?

sp.speaker: Bia.
sp.l: Which I find
Now falſe, and therefore come to make amends
I' the firſt place. I ſtand to the old conditions.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Faith give 'hem him, Sir Moath, what ere they were.
You have a brave occaſion now, to croſſe
The flanting Mr. Compaſſe, who pretends
Right to the portion, by th'other Intaile.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: And claimes it. You doe heare he's married?

sp.speaker: Bia.
sp.l: We heare his wife is run away from him,
Within: She is not to be found i'the houſe,
With all the Hue, and Cry is made for her,
Through every roome; the Larders ha' beene ſearch'd,
The Bak-houſes, and Boulting-tub, the Ovens,
Waſh-houſe, and Brew-houſe, nay the very Fornace,
And yet ſhe is not heard of.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Be ſhe nere heard of,
The ſafety of Great Brittaine lyes not on't.
You are concent with the ten thouſand pound,
Defalking the foure hundred garniſh money?
That's the condition here, afore the Doctor,
And your demand, friend Bias.

sp.speaker: Bia.
sp.l: It is Sir Moath. Enter Palate.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Here comes the Parſon then, ſhall make all ſure.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Goe you with my friend Bias, Parſon Palate,
Vnto my Neice; aſſure them wee are agreed.

sp.speaker: Pal.
sp.l: And Mrs. Compaſſe too, is found within.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Where was ſhe hid?

sp.speaker: Pal.
sp.l: In an old Botle-houſe,
Where they ſcrap'd trenchers; there her mother had thruſt her.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: You ſhall have time, Sir, to triumph on him,
When this fine feate is done, and his Rud-Ironſide.

div.head: ACT V. SCENE IX.
Compaſſe. Pleaſance. Lady. Ironſide. Practiſe. Poliſh. Chaire. Keepe. &c.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Was ever any Gentlewoman us'd
So barbarouſly by a malitious Goſſip,
Pretending to be Mother to her too?

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Pretending! Sir, I am her Mother, and challenge
A right, and power for what I have done.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Out, Hag.
Thou that haſt put all nature off, and woman:
For ſordid gaine, betray'd the truſt committed
Vnto thee by the dead, as from the living:
Chang'd the poore innocent Infants in their Cradles:
Defrauded them o' their parents, chang'd their names,
Calling Placentia, Pleaſance; Pleaſance, Placentia.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: How knowes he this?

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Abus'd the neighbour-hond,
But moſt this Lady. Did'ſt enforce an oath,
To this poore woman, on a pious booke,
To keepe cloſe thy impiety.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Ha' you told this?

sp.speaker: Kee.
sp.l: I told it? no, he knowes it, and much more,
As he's a cunning man.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: A cunning foole,
If that be all.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: But now to your true daughter,
That had the Child, and is the proper Pleaſance,
Wee muſt have an account of that too, Goſſip;

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: This's like all the reſt of Mr. Compaſſe.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Helpe, helpe for Charity; Sir Moath Intereſt
Is falne into the Well.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: Where? where?

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: I' the Garden.
A rope to ſave his life.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: How came be there?

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: He thought to take poſſeſſion of a fortune,
There newly drop't him, and the old Chaine broke,
And downe fell hee i' the Bucket.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Is it deepe?

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: We cannot tell. A rope: helpe with a rope.

sp.speaker: Sil.
sp.l: He is got out againe. The Knight is ſav'd. Enter Silke-worme. Ironſide. Item. Needle, and Intereſt. Rut.

sp.speaker: Iro.
sp.l: A little ſows'd i' the water: Needle ſav'd him.

sp.speaker: Ite.
sp.l: The water ſav'd him, 'twas a faire eſcape.

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: Ha' you no hurt?

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: A little wet.

sp.speaker: Nee.
sp.l: That's nothing.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: I wiſh'd you ſtay Sir till to morrow: And told you,
It was no lucky houre: ſince ſixe a Clock
All ſtarres were retrograde.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: I' the name Lady.
Of fate, or folly how came you i' the Bucket?

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: That is a Quere of another time, ſiſter,
The Doctor will reſolve you—who hath done
The admirable'ſt cure upon your Needle!
Gi' me thy hand good Needle: thou cam'ſt timely.
Take off my hood and coat. And let me ſhake
My ſelfe a little. I have a world of buſines. Bias. Placentia.
Where is my Nephew Bias? and his wife?
Who bids God gi'hem joy? Here they both ſtand Palate.
As ſure affianced, as the Parſon, or words
Can tie 'hem.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Wee all wiſh 'hem joy, and happineſſe.

sp.speaker: Silk.
sp.l: I ſaw the Contract, and can witneſſe it.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: He ſhall receive ten thouſand pounds to morrow.
You look'd for't, Compaſſe, or a greater ſumme,
But 'tis diſpos'd of, this, another way.
I have but one Neice, verely Compaſſe.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Ile find another. Varlet, doe your office.

sp.speaker: Var.
sp.l: I doe arreſt your body, Sir Moath Intereſt, Varlet.
In the Kings name: At ſuite of Mr. Compaſſe,
And Dame Placentia his wife. The Action sentred,
Five hundred thouſand pound.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Heare you this, ſiſter?
And hath your houſe the eares, to heare it too?
And to reſound the affront?

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: I cannot ſtop
The Lawes, or hinder Juſtice. I can be
Your Baile, if't may betaken,

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: With the Captaines,
I aske no better.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: Here are better men,
Will give their Baile.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: But yours will not be taken,
Worſhipfull Doctor; you are good ſecurity
For a ſuit of clothes, to th' Taylor, that dares truſt you:
But not for ſuch a ſumme, as is this Action.
Varlet, You know my mind.

sp.speaker: Var.
sp.l: You muſt to priſon, Sir,
Vnleſſe you can find Baile the Creditor likes.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: I would faine find it, if you'd ſhew me where.

sp.speaker: Silk.
sp.l: It is a terrible Action; more indeed,
Then many a man is worth. And is call'd Fright-Baile.

sp.speaker: Iro.
sp.l: Faith I will baile him, at mine owne apperill.
Varlet, be gone: Ile once ha'the reputation,
To be ſecurity for ſuch a ſumme.
Beare up Sir Moath.

sp.speaker: Rut.
sp.l: He is not worth the Buckles
About his Belt, and yet this Ironſide claſhes.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Peace, leſt he heare you Doctor; wee'll make uſe of him.
What doth your brother Compaſſe, Captaine Ironſide,
Demand of us, by way of challenge, thus?

sp.speaker: Iro.
sp.l: Your Neices portion; in the right of his wife.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: I have aſſur'd one portion, to one Neice,
And have no more t'account for, that I know of:
What I may doe in charity—if my ſiſter,
Will bid an Offring for her maid, and him,
As a Benevolence to 'hem, after Supper,
Ile ſpit into the Baſon, and intreat
My friends to doe the like.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Spit out thy gall,
And heart, thou Viper: I will now no mercy,
No pitty of thee, thy falſe Neice, and Needle;
Bring forth your Child, or I appeale you of murder,
You, and this Goſſip here, and Mother Chaire.

sp.speaker: Cha.
sp.l: Pleaſance ſteps out. The Gentleman's falne mad!

sp.speaker: Ple.
sp.l: No, Mrs. Midwife.
I ſaw the Child, and you did give it me,
And put it i' my armes, by this ill token,
You wiſh'd me ſuch another; and it cry'd.

sp.speaker: Pra.
sp.l: The Law is plaine; if it were heard to cry,
And you produce it not, hee may indict
All that conceale 't, of Felony, and Murder.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: And I will take the boldneſſe, Sir, to doe it:
Beginning with Sir Moath here, and his Doctor.

sp.speaker: Silk.
sp.l: Good faith this ſame is like to turne a buſines.

sp.speaker: Pal.
sp.l: And a ſhrewd buſines, marry: they all ſtart at't.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: I ha' the right thred now, and I will keepe it.
You good'y Keepe, confeſſe the truth to my Lady,
The truth, the whole truth, nothing but the truth.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: I ſcorne to be prevented of my glories.
I plotted the deceit, and I will owne it.
Love to my Child, and lucre of the portion
Provok'd me; wherein though th'event hath fail'd
In part, I will make uſe of the beſt ſide.
This is my Daughter, and ſhe hath had a Child
This day, unto her ſhame, I now profeſſe it.)
By this meere falſe-ſtick Squire Needle, but
Since this wiſe Knight, hath thought it good to change
The fooliſh Father of it, by aſſuring
Her to his deare friend, Mr. Bias; and him
Againe to her, by clapping of him on
With his free promiſe of ten thouſand pound,
Afore ſo many witneſſes.

sp.speaker: Silk.
sp.l: Whereof I
Am one.

sp.speaker: Pal.
sp.l: And I another.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: I ſhould be unnaturall
To my owne fleſh, and blood, would I not thanke him.
I thanke you Sir: and I have reaſon for it.
For here your true Neice ſtands, fine Mrs. Compaſſe.
(Ile tell you truth, you have deſerv'd it from me.)
To whom you are by bond engag'd to pay
The ſixteene thouſand pound, which is her portion,
Due to her husband, on her marriage-day.
I ſpeake the truth, and nothing but the truth.

sp.speaker: Iro.
sp.l: You'll pay it now, Sir Moath, with intereſt?
You ſee the truth breaks out on every ſide of you.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Into what nets of cous'nage am I caſt
On ev'ry ſide? each thred is growne a noofe:
A very meſh: I have run my ſelfe into
A double breake, of paying twice the money.

sp.speaker: Bia.
sp.l: You ſhall be releas'd of paying me a penny,
With theſe conditions.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Will you leave her then?

sp.speaker: Bia.
sp.l: Yes, and the ſumme, twice told, ere take a wife,
To pick out Mounſieur Needles baſting threds.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: Goſſip you are paid: though he be a fit nature,
Worthy to have a Whore juſtly put on him;
He is not bad enough to take your Daughter,
On ſuch a cheat. Will you yet pay the portion?

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: What will you 'bate?

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: No penny the Law gives.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Yes, Bias's money.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: What? your friend in Court?
I will not rob you of him, nor the purchaſe,
Nor your deare Doctor here, ſtand altogether.
Birds of a nature all, and of a feather.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: Well, wee are all now reconcil'd to truth.
There reſts yet a Gratuitie from me,
To be conferr'd upon this Gentleman;
Who (as my Nephew Compaſſe ſayes) was cauſe,
Firſt of th' offence, but ſince of all th' amends,
The Quarrell caus'd th' affright; that fright brought on
The travell, which made peace; the peace drew on
This new diſcovery, which endeth all
In reconcilement.

sp.speaker: Com.
sp.l: When the portion
Is tender'd, and receiv'd.

sp.speaker: Int.
sp.l: Well, you muſt have it,
As good at firſt as laſt. 'Tis well ſaid brother.
And I, if this good Captaine will accept me,
Give him my ſelfe, endow him with my eſtate,
And make him Lord of me, and all my fortunes:
He that hath ſav'd my houre, though by chance,
Ile really ſtudy his, and how to thanke him.

sp.speaker: Iro.
sp.l: And • imbrace you, Lady, and your goodneſſe,
And vow to quit all thought of warre hereafter;
Save what is fought under your colours, Madam.

sp.speaker: Pal.
sp.l: More worke then for the Parſon; I ſhall cap
The Loadſtone with an Ironſide, I ſee.

sp.speaker: Iro.
sp.l: And take in theſe, the forlorne Couple, with us,
Needle, and's Thred, whoſe portion I will thinke on;
As being a buſines, waiting on my bounty:
Thus I doe take poſſeſſion of you, Madam,
My true Magnetick Miſtris, and my Lady.
